subject: Chemistry
text: Galvanic cells: A galvanic cell is a device in which chemical energy is converted into electrical energy. e.g. Daniell cell.
text_html: Galvanic cells: A galvanic cell is a device in which chemical energy is converted into electrical energy. e.g. Daniell cell.

subject: Chemistry
text: Daniell cell consists of two beakers containing CuSO 4 and ZnSO 4 solutions. A zinc rod is dipped into ZnSO 4 while a copper rod is dipped into CuSO 4 solution. In this cell zinc reacts with copper (II) ions and produces metallic copper and zinc (II) ion according to the reaction: Zn(s) + Cu 2+ (aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn 2+ (aq) + Cu(s)
text_html:
Daniell cell consists of two beakers containing CuSO4 and ZnSO4 solutions. A zinc rod is dipped into ZnSO4 while a copper rod is dipped into CuSO4 solution. In this cell zinc reacts with copper (II) ions and produces metallic copper and zinc (II) ion according to the reaction:
Zn(s) + Cu2+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+(aq) + Cu(s)

text: Oxidation potential: The tendency of an electrode to lose electrons or to get oxidised is called oxidation potential. For example, M(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} M n+ (aq) + ne -

subject: Chemistry
text: Reduction potential: The tendency of an electrode to gain electrons or to get reduced is called reduction potential. For example, M n (aq) + ne - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} M(s) According to latest IUPAC convention, the half reactions are always written as reduction half reaction and their potentials are represented by reduction potentials {tex}\mathbf{E}_{o x}^o=-\mathbf{E}_{\text {red }}^o{/tex}

For example, Mn(aq) + ne- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} M(s)
According to latest IUPAC convention, the half reactions are always written as reduction half reaction and their potentials are represented by reduction potentials {tex}\mathbf{E}_{o x}^o=-\mathbf{E}_{\text {red }}^o{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Half cell potential or electrode potential of {tex}\frac {{M}^{n+}} {M}{/tex} cannot measured directly because a half cell whether oxidation or reduction half cell cannot work on its own we cannot determine the absolute electrode potential of an electrode. To solve this problem, a reference electrode standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) or normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) is used, its standard electrode potential (oxidation as well as reduction) is arbitrarily taken as zero.
text_html: Half cell potential or electrode potential of {tex}\frac {{M}^{n+}} {M}{/tex} cannot measured directly because a half cell whether oxidation or reduction half cell cannot work on its own we cannot determine the absolute electrode potential of an electrode. To solve this problem, a reference electrode standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) or normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) is used, its standard electrode potential (oxidation as well as reduction) is arbitrarily taken as zero.

subject: Chemistry
text: Electrochemical series or e.m.f. series: Arrangement of different electrodes in the order (increasing or decreasing) of their standard electrode potentials.
text_html: Electrochemical series or e.m.f. series: Arrangement of different electrodes in the order (increasing or decreasing) of their standard electrode potentials.

subject: Chemistry
text: Applications of the electrochemical series: To compare the relative oxidising and reducing powers: substances with higher reduction potentials are stronger oxidising agents. Calculation of standard emf of electrochemical cell {tex}\mathbf{E}_{\text {cell }}^\theta{/tex} : {tex}\mathbf{E}_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex} = {tex}{E}_{\text {Cathode }}^o-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^o{/tex} Comparison of the reactivity of metals: A metal with smaller reduction potential has a strong tendency to get oxidised and can displace metals having greater reduction potential from the aqueous solution of their salts. To predict whether a metal displace hydrogen from acids: Metals having a negative reduction potential value can displace hydrogen from acid. To predict the spontaneity of a redox reaction: E cell should be positive. If emf comes out to be negative, the direct reaction as given cannot take place, the reverse reaction may takes place.
text_html:
Applications of the electrochemical series:

  1. To compare the relative oxidising and reducing powers: substances with higher reduction potentials are stronger oxidising agents.
  2. Calculation of standard emf of electrochemical cell {tex}\mathbf{E}_{\text {cell }}^\theta{/tex}:
    {tex}\mathbf{E}_{\text {cell }}^o{/tex} = {tex}{E}_{\text {Cathode }}^o-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^o{/tex}
  3. Comparison of the reactivity of metals: A metal with smaller reduction potential has a strong tendency to get oxidised and can displace metals having greater reduction potential from the aqueous solution of their salts.
  4. To predict whether a metal displace hydrogen from acids: Metals having a negative reduction potential value can displace hydrogen from acid.
  5. To predict the spontaneity of a redox reaction: Ecell should be positive. If emf comes out to be negative, the direct reaction as given cannot take place, the reverse reaction may takes place.

subject: Chemistry
text: Effect of opposing potential on the cell reaction Consider a Daniell cell: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}_{(\mathrm{s})} \mid \mathrm{Zn}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+} \mathrm{\mid \mid CC}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+} \mathrm{\mid Cu}{/tex} When Subscript E ext <1.1V, Electrons flow from Zn rod to Cu rod hence current flows from Cu to Zn. When E ext >1.1 V, flow of current in this case occurs from zinc electrode to copper electrode, the cell now electrolytic cell.
text_html:
Effect of opposing potential on the cell reaction
Consider a Daniell cell: {tex}\mathrm{Zn}_{(\mathrm{s})} \mid \mathrm{Zn}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+} \mathrm{\mid \mid CC}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{2+} \mathrm{\mid Cu}{/tex}

  • When Subscript Eext <1.1V, Electrons flow from Zn rod to Cu rod hence current flows from Cu to Zn.
  • When Eext >1.1 V, flow of current in this case occurs from zinc electrode to copper electrode, the cell now electrolytic cell.

text: Nernst Equation for Single Electrode For the electrode reaction M n+ (aq) + ne - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} M(s) {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{n+} / \mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+} / \mathrm{M}}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{RT}}{{nF}} \ln \frac{\left[{M}_{({s})]}\right.}{\left[{M}^{{n+}}({aq})\right]}{/tex} Concentration of pure solid [M] should be taken as unity {tex}{E}_{{M}^{n+}/ {M}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^n / \mathrm{M}}^o{/tex} - {tex}\frac{2.303 {RT}}{{nF}} \log \frac{1}{\left[{M}^{n+}\right]}{/tex} (since log e = 2.303 log 10 ) At 298 K {tex}{E}_{{M}^{n+} /{M}}{/tex} = {tex}{E}_{{M}^{n+}+{M}}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{{n}} \log \quad \frac{1}{\left[{M}^{n+1}\right]}{/tex}
text_html:
Nernst Equation for Single Electrode
For the electrode reaction Mn+(aq) + ne- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} M(s)
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{n+} / \mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+} / \mathrm{M}}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{{RT}}{{nF}} \ln \frac{\left[{M}_{({s})]}\right.}{\left[{M}^{{n+}}({aq})\right]}{/tex}
Concentration of pure solid [M] should be taken as unity
{tex}{E}_{{M}^{n+}/ {M}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{M}^n / \mathrm{M}}^o{/tex} - {tex}\frac{2.303 {RT}}{{nF}} \log \frac{1}{\left[{M}^{n+}\right]}{/tex} (since loge = 2.303 log10)
At 298 K
{tex}{E}_{{M}^{n+} /{M}}{/tex} = {tex}{E}_{{M}^{n+}+{M}}^0{/tex} - {tex}\frac{0.059}{{n}} \log \quad \frac{1}{\left[{M}^{n+1}\right]}{/tex}

text: Electrode Potential by Nernst Equation The electrode potential at any concentration can be computed using: {tex} E_{(M^{n+}/M)}=E_{(M^{n+}/M)}^o-\frac{RT}{nF}\ln \frac{{[M]}}{[M^{n+}]} {/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Electrode Potential of Daniell Cell For a Daniell cell, Nernst provides the potentials as: Cathode: {tex}E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}=E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}^{o}-\frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{1}{[Cu^{2+}(aq)]}{/tex} Anode: {tex}E_{(Zn^{2+}/Zn)}=E_{(Zn^{2+}/Zn)}^{o}-\frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{1}{[Zn^{2+}(aq)]}{/tex}
text_html:
Electrode Potential of Daniell Cell
For a Daniell cell, Nernst provides the potentials as:

  • Cathode:


{tex}E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}=E_{(Cu^{2+}/Cu)}^{o}-\frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{1}{[Cu^{2+}(aq)]}{/tex}

 

  • Anode:

{tex}E_{(Zn^{2+}/Zn)}=E_{(Zn^{2+}/Zn)}^{o}-\frac{RT}{2F} \ln \frac{1}{[Zn^{2+}(aq)]}{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Influence of Concentration on Cell Potential Cell potential depends on the concentrations of {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex} and {tex}{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} . It increases with increasing {tex}{Cu}^{2+}{/tex} and decreasing {tex}{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} .

subject: Chemistry
text: Reasons for increase of {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} with dilution (Molar conductivity of a strong electrolyte increases with dilution because interionic attractions decrease with dilution. Small deviations at higher concentration are due to large interionic attractions.) (Molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte increases with dilution because dissociation increases with dilution.) Inability to determine limiting molar conductivity experimentally for a weak electrolyte . Molar conductivity at infinite dilution for a strong electrolyte can be found by extrapolation to zero concentration but that of weak electrolyte cannot be thus found.
text_html:
Reasons for increase of {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} with dilution
(Molar conductivity of a strong electrolyte increases with dilution because interionic attractions decrease with dilution. Small deviations at higher concentration are due to large interionic attractions.) (Molar conductivity of a weak electrolyte increases with dilution because dissociation increases with dilution.)
Inability to determine limiting molar conductivity experimentally for a weak electrolyte. Molar conductivity at infinite dilution for a strong electrolyte can be found by extrapolation to zero concentration but that of weak electrolyte cannot be thus found.

subject: Chemistry
text: KOHLRAUSCH LAW OF INDEPENDENT MIGRATION OFIONS The law states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte. For e.g. Al 2 (SO 4 ) 3 {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left[\mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3\right]{/tex} = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right)+3 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}\right){/tex}
text_html:
KOHLRAUSCH LAW OF INDEPENDENT MIGRATION OFIONS
The law states that limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte can be represented as the sum of the individual contributions of the anion and cation of the electrolyte.
For e.g. Al2(SO4)3 {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left[\mathrm{Al}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3\right]{/tex} = {tex}2 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{Al}^{3+}\right)+3 \lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^0\left(\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}\right){/tex}

subject: Chemistry
text: Applications of Kohlrausch's Law In calculation of limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolyte In calculation of degree of dissociation, i.e., {tex}\alpha= \frac {\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}}{ \wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0}{/tex} In calculation of dissociation constant (K c ) by using value of a, K c = {tex}\frac {c \alpha^2}{ 1-\alpha}{/tex}
text_html:
Applications of Kohlrausch's Law

  1. In calculation of limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolyte
  2. In calculation of degree of dissociation, i.e., {tex}\alpha= \frac {\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}}{ \wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^0}{/tex}
  3. In calculation of dissociation constant (Kc) by using value of a, Kc = {tex}\frac {c \alpha^2}{ 1-\alpha}{/tex}

text: Mechnism of Rusting Electrochemical theory of rusting: Impure surface of iron act as an electrochenical cell. Pure Iron act as anode and impure iron as cathode. Carbonic acid act as the electrolyte (It provide H + ion). H 2 O + CO 2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H 2 CO 3 {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2H + + CO 3 2- Setting up of Electrochemical cell on Iron surface Fe(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe 2+ + 2e - (Anode) E o = -0.44 V O 2 + 4H + + 2H 2 O (Cathode) E o = +1.23 V 2Fe(s) + O 2 + 4H + {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Fe 2+ + 2H 2 O E cell = 1.67 V Cathode is further oxidised by atmospheric oxygen to form rust. Fe 2 O 3 .xH 2 O 4Fe 2+ + O 2 + 4H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Fe 2 O 3 + 8H + Fe 2 O 3 + xH 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe 2 O 3 {tex}\cdot{/tex} xH 2 O (Rust)

Mechnism of Rusting
Electrochemical theory of rusting:
Impure surface of iron act as an electrochenical cell. Pure Iron act as anode and impure iron as cathode. Carbonic acid act as the electrolyte (It provide H+ ion).

  1. H2O + CO2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} H2CO3 {tex}\leftrightharpoons{/tex} 2H+ + CO32-
    Setting up of Electrochemical cell on Iron surface
  2. Fe(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe2+ + 2e-(Anode) Eo = -0.44 V
  3. O2 + 4H+ + 2H2O (Cathode) Eo = +1.23 V
    2Fe(s) + O2 + 4H+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Fe2+ + 2H2O Ecell = 1.67 V
    Cathode is further oxidised by atmospheric oxygen to form rust. Fe2 O3.xH2O
  4. 4Fe2+ + O2 + 4H2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2 Fe2O3 + 8H+
    Fe2O3 + xH2O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Fe2O3{tex}\cdot{/tex}xH2O (Rust)

text: The Chemistry of Corrosion Corrosion is essentially an electrochemical phenomenon, involving oxidation as the metal loses electrons to form oxides. text_html:
The Chemistry of Corrosion
Corrosion is essentially an electrochemical phenomenon, involving oxidation as the metal loses electrons to form oxides. In the case of iron:
  • Oxidation takes place at the anode where iron is converted to ferrous ions.
  • Electrons travel to another spot on the metal, reducing oxygen at the cathode.
